title: Distance and Angle Measurement
description: Field acquisition of linear and angular data: taping with all standard corrections, EDM and total-station distance measurement, horizontal/vertical/zenith angles, direct-and-reverse observation, bearings vs azimuths, DMS arithmetic, magnetic declination, and the slope-to-horizontal reduction.
key_topics: Taping corrections (temperature, tension/pull, sag, slope, standardization)
EDM principles and instrument-constant + ppm error
Bearings vs azimuths and quadrant conversion
Magnetic declination and the magnetic-to-true correction
Slope distance to horizontal distance (H = S·sin Z)

key_formulas.name: True from magnetic (east declination)
key_formulas.formula: True bearing = Magnetic bearing + East declination

content_sections.title: Module Overview
content_sections.content:
## Field Data Acquisition: Distances and Angles

Every coordinate, traverse, and boundary computation on the FS exam ultimately rests on two raw field observations: a **distance** and a **direction (angle)**. This module covers how those observations are actually made and, more importantly for the exam, how they are **corrected and reduced** into the clean numbers you later feed into COGO.

You will work through three eras of distance measurement that the exam still tests:

1. **Taping (chaining)** — and its five systematic corrections: temperature, tension/pull, sag, slope, and standardization. The exam loves a tape-correction problem because it isolates one concept and rewards careful sign handling.
2. **EDM (electronic distance measurement)** — the phase/pulse principle, the **instrument (additive) constant**, and the **atmospheric ppm** correction tied to temperature and pressure.
3. **Total stations** — which marry an EDM to an electronic theodolite, plus robotic and reflectorless variants.

On the angular side you will master **horizontal angles, vertical angles, and zenith angles**, the **direct-and-reverse** (face-left/face-right) procedure that cancels instrumental error, and the conversions that trip up examinees under time pressure: **DMS↔decimal degrees**, **azimuth↔bearing**, **back-azimuth**, and the **magnetic declination** correction for historical (record) bearings.

Finally you will reduce a **slope distance to a horizontal distance** using the zenith angle — the single most common reduction in trigonometric field work: **H = S·sin(Z)**.

Because the NCEES calculator (TI-36X Pro / Casio fx-991) does DMS entry, DMS arithmetic, and rectangular↔polar conversion natively, this module pairs every worked example with the exact keystrokes so the conversions become reflexive on exam day.

content_sections.title: Key Concepts
content_sections.content:
## 1. Taping and its corrections

A steel tape is standardized (calibrated) at a known **temperature (68°F / 20°C)**, a known **tension (pull)**, and a known **support condition** (fully supported on a flat surface, or supported only at the ends). When field conditions differ, the measured length is systematically wrong and must be corrected. The five standard corrections:

- **Temperature** — steel expands when warm. `Ct = α·(T − Ts)·L` with the coefficient of thermal expansion for steel **α = 0.00000645 / °F** (i.e. 6.45×10⁻⁶ per °F) and standard temperature **Ts = 68°F**. Hot tape reads short (so the correction is positive when T > 68°F for a measured distance).
- **Tension / pull** — pulling harder than standard stretches the tape: `Cp = (P − P0)·L / (A·E)`, where A = cross-sectional area, E = modulus of elasticity (≈29,000,000 psi for steel).
- **Sag** — an unsupported tape hangs in a catenary and reads **long**, so the correction is always **negative**: `Cs = −w²·L³ / (24·P²)`, w = weight per foot, P = applied tension.
- **Slope** — a tape stretched along a slope measures the slope distance, not the horizontal. The reduction `Ch ≈ −h²/(2L)` (h = elevation difference) or exactly `H = S·cos(vertical angle)`.
- **Standardization** — the tape's true length differs from its nominal length (e.g. a "100 ft" tape is actually 100.012 ft). Add the per-tape-length error proportionally.

**Sign discipline is everything.** Decide whether you are (a) *measuring a distance in the field* or (b) *laying out a distance to set a point* — the sign of every correction flips between the two cases.

## 2. EDM and total stations

An **EDM** transmits a modulated light/IR wave to a prism (or directly to a surface, *reflectorless*) and infers distance from the phase shift (or pulse time-of-flight). Two errors dominate exam problems:

- **Instrument (additive/zero) constant** — a fixed offset `a` in mm, independent of distance.
- **Scale / ppm error** — proportional to distance, expressed in **parts per million**. Atmospheric conditions matter: roughly a **10°C** temperature change **or** a **1 in Hg (≈25 mm Hg)** pressure change is about **10 ppm**. Total stated EDM precision is written as **±(a mm + b ppm)**.

A **total station** combines an EDM with an electronic (digital) theodolite and an onboard processor, outputting horizontal angle, zenith angle, and slope distance — and computing H, V, and coordinates internally. **Robotic** total stations track a prism for one-person crews; **reflectorless** units measure to a surface without a prism.

## 3. Angles: horizontal, vertical, zenith

- **Horizontal angle** — measured in the horizontal plane between two directions; the basis of traverse turning angles and angles-to-the-right.
- **Vertical angle (α)** — measured **up (+) or down (−) from the horizontal** (0° = level).
- **Zenith angle (Z)** — measured **down from the upward vertical** (zenith). A level sight = **90°**. Relationship: **Z = 90° − α**. Most modern total stations read **zenith** angles.

## 4. Direct and reverse (D&R)

Observing an angle in both telescope positions — **direct (face left / face I)** and **reverse (face right / face II, telescope plunged)** — and **meaning** the two cancels instrumental errors (collimation, horizontal-axis tilt, vertical-circle index). The mean of D&R is the accepted angle; the **2C** (direct + reverse − 360°) checks collimation.

## 5. Directions: azimuths, bearings, declination

- **Azimuth** — clockwise angle from a reference meridian (usually north), **0°–360°**.
- **Bearing** — acute angle (0°–90°) from north OR south, toward east or west, written as a quadrant call (e.g. **N42°15′E**, **S08°40′W**).
- **Back-azimuth** = Az ± 180° (use + if Az < 180°, − if Az ≥ 180°). **Back-bearing** = same angle, opposite quadrant letters.
- **Magnetic declination** — the angle between magnetic north and true (geodetic) north at a place and date. **East declination is added** to a magnetic bearing to get true; **west declination is subtracted**. Converting old magnetic deed calls to true direction (and accounting for *secular change* in declination between two dates) is a classic boundary/retracement question.

content_sections.title: Essential Formulas
content_sections.content:
## Taping corrections (apply to a measured length L)

**Temperature (thermal):**

`Ct = 0.00000645 · (T − 68) · L`   (L, Ct in ft; T in °F; α_steel = 6.45×10⁻⁶/°F)

Metric form: `Ct = 0.0000116 · (T − 20) · L` (T in °C, α = 11.6×10⁻⁶/°C).

**Tension / pull:**

`Cp = (P − P0)·L / (A·E)`   (P, P0 in lb; A in in²; E ≈ 29,000,000 psi for steel)

**Sag (always negative — tape reads long):**

`Cs = − w²·L³ / (24·P²)`   (w = weight per ft; or `Cs = −W²·L/(24·P²)` with total weight W)

**Slope reduction (slope length S → horizontal H):**

`H = S · cos(α)`   (α = vertical angle)  •  `H = S · sin(Z)`   (Z = zenith angle)

Approximate correction: `Ch ≈ − h² / (2L)`   (h = elevation difference over length L)

**Standardization:** `Cstd = (actual tape length − nominal) · (measured / nominal)`

**Corrected length:** `L_corrected = L_measured + Ct + Cp + Cs + Ch + Cstd`

## EDM

**Combined stated error / correction:**

`e(mm) = a_mm + b_ppm · D_km · 1000`   ⇒ for D in km, ppm term in mm = `b · D_km`

Equivalently, for D in meters: `e(mm) = a + b·(D/1,000,000)·... ` — simplest: ppm error (mm) = `b · D(km)`.

Atmospheric rule of thumb: ≈ **10 ppm per 10°C** or per **1 in Hg** pressure difference.

## Angles

`Z = 90° − α`   (zenith ↔ vertical angle)

`2C = (Direct + Reverse) − 360°`   (collimation check; mean angle = (D + (R−180°))/2)

## DMS ↔ decimal degrees

`dd = D + M/60 + S/3600`

`D = trunc(dd)` ; `M = trunc((dd − D)·60)` ; `S = (((dd − D)·60) − M)·60`

Total seconds: `total" = D·3600 + M·60 + S`

## Azimuth ↔ bearing (by quadrant of the azimuth)

| Quadrant | Azimuth range | Bearing | Az→Brg | Brg→Az |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| I — NE | 0°–90° | N (Az) E | brg = Az | Az = brg |
| II — SE | 90°–180° | S (180−Az) E | brg = 180 − Az | Az = 180 − brg |
| III — SW | 180°–270° | S (Az−180) W | brg = Az − 180 | Az = 180 + brg |
| IV — NW | 270°–360° | N (360−Az) W | brg = 360 − Az | Az = 360 − brg |

**Back-azimuth:** `Back-Az = Az + 180°` (if Az < 180°) or `Az − 180°` (if Az ≥ 180°).

## Magnetic declination

`True = Magnetic + East_declination`  •  `True = Magnetic − West_declination`

Secular change between dates: apply the *difference* in declination to convert an old magnetic call to a present-day true (or current magnetic) direction.

content_sections.title: Worked Examples
content_sections.content:
## Example 1 — Temperature tape correction

A line is measured with a steel tape standardized at 68°F as **measured length = 842.60 ft**. The field temperature was **96°F**. What is the corrected horizontal length (tape fully supported, standard tension)?

**Solution.** Use `Ct = 0.00000645·(T − 68)·L`:

`Ct = 0.00000645 × (96 − 68) × 842.60 = 0.00000645 × 28 × 842.60 = +0.152 ft`

The tape was hot, so it read short; the true distance is longer:

`L_corrected = 842.60 + 0.152 = 842.75 ft`

**TI-36X Pro keystrokes:** `0.00000645 × ( 96 − 68 ) × 842.60 =` → 0.15225. Then `842.60 + 0.15225 =`. (Enter 6.45E-6 with the `×10ⁿ` / `EE` key: `6.45` `×10ⁿ` `(-) 6`.)

**Casio fx-991:** identical; use the `×10ˣ` key for the exponent: `6.45` `×10ˣ` `(−)6`.

---

## Example 2 — Slope distance → horizontal distance (zenith angle)

A total station reads a **slope distance S = 415.270 ft** to a prism with a **zenith angle Z = 87°12′30″**. What is the horizontal distance?

**Solution.** `H = S · sin(Z)`. First get Z into the calculator in DMS:

Enter `87°12′30″` using the DMS key, then `H = 415.270 × sin(Z)`.

`sin(87°12′30″) = sin(87.20833°) = 0.998813`

`H = 415.270 × 0.998813 = 414.777 ft`

**TI-36X Pro keystrokes:** put calc in **DEG** mode. `415.270 × sin( 87 [°’”] 12 [°’”] 30 [°’”] ) =` → 414.777. The `[°’”]` key is `2nd` `[matrix/angle]` → `°’”` (the DMS template). You may also type 87 `°’”` 12 `°’”` 30 `°’”`.

**Casio fx-991:** `415.270 × sin( 87 [°’’’] 12 [°’’’] 30 [°’’’] ) =`. The `°’’’` key enters and displays sexagesimal directly; pressing `=` then `°’’’` toggles a result between decimal and DMS.

*Check with the vertical angle:* α = 90° − 87°12′30″ = 2°47′30″, and `H = S·cos(α) = 415.270 × cos(2.79167°) = 414.777 ft`. ✓

---

## Example 3 — DMS arithmetic and DMS↔decimal

**(a)** Convert **218°43′52″** to decimal degrees.

`dd = 218 + 43/60 + 52/3600 = 218 + 0.716667 + 0.014444 = 218.731111°`

**(b)** Add two angles: **47°38′45″ + 22°51′30″**.

Seconds: 45 + 30 = 75″ → 1′15″. Minutes: 38 + 51 + 1 = 90′ → 1°30′. Degrees: 47 + 22 + 1 = 70°. Result = **70°30′15″**.

**TI-36X Pro:** `47 °’” 38 °’” 45 °’” + 22 °’” 51 °’” 30 °’” =` displays **70°30′15″** directly (the calculator carries the base-60 arithmetic). Press `2nd` `[°’”]` (the `▸DMS`/`▸DD` toggle) to flip the answer to decimal.

**Casio fx-991:** `47°’’’38°’’’45°’’’ + 22°’’’51°’’’30°’’’ =` → `70°30′15″`. Press `°’’’` again to read 70.50416667°.

---

## Example 4 — Azimuth ↔ bearing and back-azimuth

A line has **azimuth 218°43′52″**. Find its bearing and its back-azimuth.

**Bearing:** azimuth is in **Quadrant III (180°–270°, SW)**, so `bearing = Az − 180° = 218°43′52″ − 180° = 38°43′52″`, written **S 38°43′52″ W**.

**Back-azimuth:** Az ≥ 180°, so `Back-Az = 218°43′52″ − 180° = 38°43′52″`.

*(Sanity check: the back-azimuth's bearing is N 38°43′52″ E — the exact reverse quadrant of S 38°43′52″ W. ✓)*

---

## Example 5 — EDM constant + ppm error

An EDM is rated **±(2 mm + 2 ppm)**. For a measured distance **D = 1,850 m**, what is the worst-case standard error in mm?

**Solution.** ppm term = `b · D(km) = 2 × 1.850 = 3.70 mm`. Constant term = 2 mm.

`e = a + b·D(km) = 2 + 2(1.850) = 2 + 3.70 = 5.70 mm`

So the line is good to about **±5.7 mm** (≈ ±0.006 m).

**Calculator:** trivial — `2 + 2 × 1.850 =`. The trap is unit handling: convert D to **km** before multiplying by the ppm figure (2 ppm of 1,850,000 mm = 3.7 mm).

---

## Example 6 — Magnetic declination (retracement)

An 1885 deed calls a line **N 12°30′ E (magnetic)**. The magnetic declination at that place and date was **8°15′ East**. What is the **true** bearing of the line?

**Solution.** East declination is **added** to a magnetic bearing measured toward the east:

`True = N (12°30′ + 8°15′) E = N 20°45′ E`

If you then needed to re-run the line today with a compass, you would apply the *present-day* declination (and its sign) instead, accounting for secular change.

content_sections.title: Exam Tips & Calculator Strategy
content_sections.content:
## Exam strategy for distance & angle items

- **Read for the reduction asked.** Many problems give a slope distance and a zenith angle and ask for *horizontal distance* — that is just `H = S·sin(Z)`. Don't over-build it.
- **Sign of every correction.** Decide first: am I *measuring* (correcting a field reading) or *laying out* (setting a point)? Sag is **always negative**; temperature follows the sign of (T − 68); slope reduction always *shortens* slope to horizontal.
- **Zenith vs vertical angle.** A level sight is **Z = 90°** and **α = 0°**. If a problem gives a "vertical angle" near 0° use `cos`; if it gives a "zenith angle" near 90° use `sin`. Mixing them is the #1 trap. `Z = 90° − α`.
- **EDM ppm needs km.** Convert the distance to kilometers before multiplying by the ppm rating (2 ppm × 1.85 km = 3.7 mm). 10 ppm ≈ 10°C or 1 in Hg of atmosphere.
- **Quadrant before conversion.** For azimuth↔bearing, identify the quadrant from the azimuth first, then apply the matching rule (NE: same; SE: 180−; SW: −180; NW: 360−). Back-azimuth is ±180°.
- **Declination direction.** "East is least, west is best" is backwards for *magnetic→true*: for a true-from-magnetic conversion, **add east declination, subtract west**. Watch whether the problem wants magnetic→true or true→magnetic (the sign flips).

## Approved-calculator tricks (TI-36X Pro / Casio fx-991)

- **DMS entry & arithmetic:** Use the **°’”** (TI) / **°’’’** (Casio) key to enter angles directly as D°M'S". The calculator does base-60 add/subtract/multiply for you — never convert to decimal by hand on the exam unless asked. Toggle a result between DMS and decimal with `2nd °’”` (TI) or by pressing `°’’’` again (Casio).
- **DEG mode:** confirm the calculator is in **degrees** (not RAD/GRAD) before any trig — a wrong angle mode silently corrupts every answer.
- **R▸P / P▸R (rectangular↔polar):** for inverse-type and forward-coordinate work, `R▸P` converts (ΔE, ΔN) → (distance, azimuth) in one step, and `P▸R` converts (distance, azimuth) → (ΔE, ΔN). On the TI-36X Pro these live under `2nd` `[R▸P]`/`[P▸R]`; on the Casio fx-991 use `Pol(` and `Rec(`. This is the fastest path for slope/azimuth bookkeeping and carries straight into the next module's COGO.
- **HP-33S/35S (RPN, if used):** `→POL`/`→REC` and `→HMS`/`→HR` give the same conversions; enter DMS as `D.MMSSss` and use `→HR` to decimalize.
- **Store-and-recall:** stash a standardized-tape length or a declination value in memory so you don't re-key it across a multi-part problem.

## How the handbook helps

The NCEES FS Reference Handbook gives you the EDM relationship (V = c/n, λ = V/f), the curvature-&-refraction constants, the azimuth/bearing quadrant constants (C = 0/180/180/360), and the inverse/forward direction formulas verbatim. Know *where* taping corrections and the slope reduction live so you can confirm a coefficient under time pressure instead of trusting memory. Practice every conversion on the actual handbook PDF before exam day.

questions.question_text: A steel tape is standardized at 68°F. The coefficient of thermal expansion for steel used on the FS is most nearly:
questions.explanation: For steel, α ≈ 6.45×10⁻⁶ per °F (the value 11.6×10⁻⁶ is the per-°C coefficient). The temperature correction is Ct = 0.00000645·(T − 68)·L.

questions.question_text: A 100.00-ft measured distance is taped at 103°F with a tape standardized at 68°F. The temperature correction is most nearly:
questions.explanation: Ct = 0.00000645·(103 − 68)·100.00 = 0.00000645·35·100 = +0.0226 ft. Positive because the warm tape reads short.

questions.question_text: The sag correction applied to a tape suspended only at its ends is:
options: Zero if tension equals the standard pull
Equal in magnitude to the slope correction
questions.explanation: An unsupported tape hangs as a catenary, so the measured distance is longer than the true horizontal chord; the correction Cs = −w²L³/(24P²) is always subtractive.

questions.question_text: A slope distance of 250.00 ft is measured along a grade with a zenith angle of 92°00′00″. The horizontal distance is most nearly:
A zenith angle of 90°00′00″ corresponds to a vertical angle of:
questions.explanation: Z = 90° − α, so a level sight (Z = 90°) is a vertical angle of 0°.

questions.question_text: Which best describes the purpose of observing an angle in both direct and reverse (face-left and face-right) positions?
options: It cancels instrumental errors such as collimation and horizontal-axis tilt
It eliminates the need for a back-azimuth
It reduces a slope distance to horizontal
questions.correct_answer: It cancels instrumental errors such as collimation and horizontal-axis tilt
questions.explanation: Meaning the direct and reverse observations cancels systematic instrumental errors (collimation, trunnion-axis tilt, vertical-circle index). It has nothing to do with declination or slope reduction.

questions.question_text: An EDM is rated ±(3 mm + 2 ppm). For a 2,000-m line, the standard error is most nearly:
questions.explanation: ppm term = 2 ppm × 2.000 km = 4 mm; total e = 3 + 4 = 7 mm. (2 ppm of 2,000,000 mm = 4 mm.)

questions.question_text: Approximately what change in conditions produces a 10-ppm atmospheric correction to an EDM distance?
options: A 10°C temperature change or a 1 in Hg pressure change
A 1°C temperature change or a 10 in Hg pressure change
Any change in the prism constant
questions.correct_answer: A 10°C temperature change or a 1 in Hg pressure change
questions.explanation: As a rule of thumb, roughly a 10°C temperature difference, or a 1 in Hg (≈25 mm Hg) pressure difference, corresponds to about 10 ppm of distance correction.

questions.question_text: Convert the azimuth 312°20′00″ to a bearing.
questions.explanation: 312° is in Quadrant IV (270°–360°, NW), so bearing = 360° − Az = 360° − 312°20′ = 47°40′, written N 47°40′00″ W.

questions.question_text: Convert the bearing S 23°15′00″ E to an azimuth.
The azimuth of a line is 73°10′00″. Its back-azimuth is:
Convert 41°27′36″ to decimal degrees.
Add the angles 38°52′40″ + 16°19′35″. The sum is:
An old deed calls a line N 18°00′ W (magnetic) where the declination was 6°30′ West at the time of the survey. The true bearing is:
questions.explanation: For a NW-quadrant magnetic bearing, west declination rotates the magnetic meridian west of true, so the true bearing swings further from north: N (18°00′ + 6°30′) W = N 24°30′ W. (Carefully reason the geometry; a west declination is subtracted for an east bearing but increases a west bearing.)

questions.question_text: A total station outputs which set of raw values directly?
options: Horizontal angle, zenith angle, and slope distance
questions.correct_answer: Horizontal angle, zenith angle, and slope distance
questions.explanation: A total station marries an electronic theodolite (horizontal + zenith angles) to an EDM (slope distance); it then computes H, V, and coordinates internally.

questions.question_text: A reflectorless total station differs from a conventional total station primarily in that it:
options: Measures distance to a surface without a prism
Uses a steel tape for backup
questions.correct_answer: Measures distance to a surface without a prism
questions.explanation: Reflectorless EDM returns the signal from the target surface itself, eliminating the prism for points that are hard or unsafe to occupy.

questions.question_text: The instrument (additive) constant of an EDM is best described as:
options: A fixed offset, in mm, independent of the distance measured
An error proportional to the distance
The prism height above the point
questions.correct_answer: A fixed offset, in mm, independent of the distance measured
questions.explanation: The additive/zero constant is a constant offset (in mm) regardless of distance; the ppm/scale term is the distance-proportional part.

questions.question_text: A line is taped down a uniform 8% grade as a slope length of 300.00 ft. The horizontal distance is most nearly (use the approximate slope correction Ch ≈ −h²/2L):
Which calculator function most directly converts a distance and azimuth into a latitude (ΔN) and departure (ΔE)?
questions.explanation: P▸R converts (distance, azimuth) into the rectangular components (ΔE, ΔN). R▸P does the inverse (components → distance and azimuth).

questions.question_text: True or False: A vertical angle is measured up or down from the horizontal plane, while a zenith angle is measured down from the upward vertical.
questions.explanation: Correct. Vertical angle α = 0° at the horizon; zenith angle Z = 0° straight up and 90° at the horizon, so Z = 90° − α.

questions.question_text: True or False: The temperature correction Ct = 0.00000645·(T − 68)·L is negative when the tape is colder than 68°F.
questions.explanation: When T < 68°F, (T − 68) is negative, so Ct is negative — a cold tape has contracted and reads long, so the true measured distance is slightly shorter.

questions.question_text: A slope distance of 180.00 ft is observed with a vertical angle of +3°30′00″. The horizontal distance is most nearly:
Express 218°43′52″ in total seconds of arc.
The '2C' value used as a collimation check is computed as:
questions.explanation: For a pointing, the reverse reading should differ from the direct by 180°; 2C = (Direct + Reverse) − 360° gives twice the collimation error and should be small and consistent.

questions.question_text: For the most accurate EDM measurement over a long line on a hot day, the surveyor should primarily:
options: Record temperature and pressure to apply the atmospheric (ppm) correction
Use an umbrella to shade the instrument battery
questions.correct_answer: Record temperature and pressure to apply the atmospheric (ppm) correction
questions.explanation: The dominant variable error over a long EDM line is the atmospheric (ppm) correction, which depends on temperature and pressure; recording them lets you apply the correct ppm.

questions.question_text: A line measured with an EDM reads 1,402.118 m. The instrument's additive (prism) constant is −0.030 m. The corrected distance is:
questions.explanation: Apply the additive constant: 1,402.118 + (−0.030) = 1,402.088 m.

problem_templates.name: DMS to decimal degrees
problem_templates.question_template: Convert the angle {{D}}°{{M}}′{{S}}″ to decimal degrees.
problem_templates.explanation_template: Decimal degrees = {{D}} + {{M}}/60 + {{S}}/3600 = {{_answer}}°. On the TI-36X Pro enter the angle with the °’” key and toggle to decimal with 2nd °’”.

problem_templates.name: DMS to total seconds of arc
problem_templates.question_template: Express the angle {{D}}°{{M}}′{{S}}″ in total seconds of arc.

problem_templates.name: Azimuth to bearing
problem_templates.question_template: A line has azimuth {{az:f4}}°, which lies in the SW quadrant. Give the bearing angle (degrees from south, toward west).

solution_steps_template.title: Identify quadrant
solution_steps_template.calculation_template: Az = {{az:f4}}° is between 180° and 270° → Quadrant III (SW)

problem_templates.explanation_template: For an azimuth in the SW quadrant (180°–270°), bearing = Az − 180° = {{az:f4}} − 180 = {{_answer}}°, written S {{_answer}}° W.

problem_templates.name: Bearing to azimuth (NW quadrant)
problem_templates.question_template: A line bears N {{brg:f4}}° W. Compute its azimuth (clockwise from north, 0°–360°).
problem_templates.explanation_template: For a NW-quadrant bearing, azimuth = 360° − bearing = 360 − {{brg:f4}} = {{_answer}}°.

problem_templates.name: Back-azimuth
problem_templates.question_template: The forward azimuth of a line is {{az:f4}}°. Compute its back-azimuth (0°–360°).

solution_steps_template.title: Apply ±180° rule
solution_steps_template.calculation_template: If Az < 180°, add 180°; if Az ≥ 180°, subtract 180°

problem_templates.explanation_template: Back-azimuth = Az ± 180° = {{_answer}}° (add 180° when Az < 180°, subtract 180° when Az ≥ 180°).

problem_templates.name: Temperature tape correction
problem_templates.question_template: A distance of {{L:f2}} ft is measured with a steel tape standardized at 68°F. The field temperature is {{T}}°F. Compute the corrected length (ft). Use Ct = 0.00000645·(T − 68)·L.
problem_templates.explanation_template: Ct = 0.00000645·({{T}}−68)·{{L:f2}} = {{Ct:f3}} ft; corrected length = {{L:f2}} + {{Ct:f3}} = {{_answer}} ft. The correction is positive when T > 68°F (hot tape reads short).

problem_templates.name: Horizontal distance from slope distance and zenith angle
problem_templates.question_template: A total station measures a slope distance of {{S:f3}} ft with a zenith angle of {{Zd}}°{{Zm}}′{{Zs}}″. Compute the horizontal distance (ft). Use H = S·sin(Z).
problem_templates.explanation_template: Convert the zenith angle to {{Zdd:f4}}°, then H = S·sin(Z) = {{S:f3}}·sin({{Zdd:f4}}°) = {{_answer}} ft. Enter the angle in DMS and keep the calculator in DEG mode.

problem_templates.name: EDM constant + ppm error
problem_templates.question_template: An EDM is rated ±({{a}} mm + {{b}} ppm). For a measured distance of {{Dm}} m, compute the worst-case standard error (mm).
problem_templates.explanation_template: ppm error (mm) = b·D(km) = {{b}}·{{Dkm:f3}} = {{ppmTerm:f2}} mm; total e = a + ppm term = {{a}} + {{ppmTerm:f2}} = {{_answer}} mm. Always convert the distance to km before multiplying by the ppm rating.

mock_questions.question_text: A 500.00-ft distance is taped at 38°F with a steel tape standardized at 68°F. Using Ct = 0.00000645·(T−68)·L, the corrected length is most nearly:
A slope distance of 326.40 ft is measured with a zenith angle of 85°30′00″. The horizontal distance is most nearly:
The azimuth 248°15′00″ expressed as a bearing is:
mock_questions.explanation: 248° is in Quadrant III (SW); bearing = Az − 180° = 68°15′, written S 68°15′00″ W.

mock_questions.question_text: An EDM rated ±(2 mm + 3 ppm) measures a 1,500-m line. The expected standard error is most nearly:
mock_questions.explanation: ppm term = 3 × 1.500 km = 4.5 mm; total = 2 + 4.5 = 6.5 mm.

mock_questions.question_text: Observing a horizontal angle in both face-left and face-right and meaning the results primarily serves to:
mock_questions.explanation: Direct-and-reverse observation cancels systematic instrumental errors; it does nothing for slope, declination, or atmospheric corrections.

mock_questions.question_text: A zenith angle is measured from the upward vertical. The corresponding vertical angle for a zenith reading of 96°00′00″ is:
mock_questions.explanation: α = 90° − Z = 90° − 96° = −6° (a depression angle, sighting downhill).

mock_questions.question_text: Which instrument directly combines an electronic theodolite and an EDM with onboard computation of coordinates?
mock_questions.explanation: A total station integrates an electronic theodolite (angles) and EDM (distance) with a processor that computes H, V, and coordinates.

mock_questions.question_text: An 1870 deed line reads N 30°00′ E (magnetic); the declination then was 5°00′ East. The true bearing is:
mock_questions.explanation: For an east-quadrant bearing, east declination is added: N (30°00′ + 5°00′) E = N 35°00′ E.

mock_questions.question_text: The sag correction for a tape supported only at its ends is:
mock_questions.explanation: The catenary sag makes the measured chord longer than the true horizontal distance, so the correction Cs = −w²L³/(24P²) is subtractive.

mock_questions.question_text: The most direct way to reduce a slope distance S to a horizontal distance H given the vertical angle α is:
mock_questions.explanation: With the vertical angle measured from horizontal, the horizontal component is H = S·cos(α). (Using the zenith angle Z it is H = S·sin(Z).)

mock_questions.question_text: For best long-line EDM accuracy, the field crew should mainly record:
mock_questions.explanation: The atmospheric (ppm) correction depends on temperature and pressure; recording them lets the crew apply the correct first-velocity correction.

mock_questions.question_text: The back-azimuth of a line whose forward azimuth is 200°00′00″ is:
